content.full_text:
Farn Porrucar PnecrrcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
April 11,2005
Deborah Dahl Shanks
2698 Emma Drive
Pinole, CA 94564
Re:
Your Request for fnformal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-05-056
Dear Ms. Shanks:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding your duties as a
member of the Contra Costa Community College District's Governing Board of Trustees
(if you are elected to the.board) under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the 'Act").' Because your letter is general in nafure, we can only provide
general, informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunityprovided by an opinion or {brmal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation
1832e(c)(3).)
The Commission's advice is limited to matters arising under the Act. (Regulation
18329(bX8XD).) Other laws do restrict the ability of public officials to hold two
different public offices simultaneously if the offices are deterrnine to be "incompatible
offices." It would be advisable for you to contact the state Attorney General's office with
respect to the doctrine of incompatible offices and other provisions of law that might
apply to your facts.
QUESTTON
Does the Act bar you from serving on the Contra Costa Community College
District's Governing Board of Trustees if you are also an instructor for Diablo Valley
College which is a part of the district?
' Gorrem-ert Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, ofthe Califomia Code of Regulations.


File No. I-05-056
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
The Act does not bar you from holding both positions. However, your
employmentmay disqualiff you from decisions that affect your salary as a teacher.
FACTS
You are planning on running xtd/or serving as a Trustee for the Contra Costa
Community College District's Governing Board of Trustees. You are also a temporary,
part-time instructor at Diablo Valley College (in the Contra Costa Community College
District) and work for Foothill College (parttime). Your income from the college district
is about $2,000 per year. You state that you do not receive any benefits (health or other)
from the diskict as their anployee. You would like to continue to teach part-time (as
classes are available on an "as needed" basis) and still be able to run and, if elected serve
on the Board of Trustees.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict of interest rules prohibit public officials frorn making,
participating in making, or in any way atternpting to use their official positions to
influence goveffImental decisions in which theyhave financial interests. (Section 87100;
regulation 187000) describes in detail each step of the analysis that follows.)
Steps I &2. Are you a public official who may make, participate in making, or use
your official position to influence a governmental decision?
The Act's conflict-of-interest rules apply only to public officials. If you became a
member of the Contra Costa Community College District's Governing Board of Trustees,
you would be a public official governed by these provisions. (Section 82A48;regulations
18700(bxr), 18701.)
The prohibition of section 871 00 applies to specific conduct by public officials
making, participating in making, or using one's official position to influence a
governmental decision. (Regulations 18702.l-87A2.4-) As a member of the Contra
costa community college District's Goveming Board of Trustees, you would be
required to make, participate in making or influence particular govemmental decisions.
Therefore, if you were elected to the Board your activities would be covered by the Act,s
conflict of interest rules. (Regulation I 8700(b)(2).)
Step 3. What are your economic interests?
There are two pertinent economic interests evident from the facts you provided.
We limit our analysis to these two economic interests.
o A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 ormore within l2 months prior to the


File No. I-05-056
Page No. 3
time in which the relevant governmental decision was made (section 87103(c);
regulation 18703.3);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances and those of
his orher immediate family - this is the "personal financial effects" rule (section
87 103; regulation I 8703.5).
For purposes of the Act, section 82030(b)(2) provides that 'tncome" does not
include "salary and reimbursernent for expenses or per diem ... received from a state,
local or federal govemment agency." (See also regulation 1S705(c)(l).) BV operation of
the "government salary exception," the salaries you receive through your faculty
positions are not "income" within the meaning of the Act. Therefore, if you held a
position on the Board, you would not need to disqualifu yourself from decisions that may
affect the college in which you teach since the college would not be a source of income io
you forpurposes of the Act.
However, notwithstanding the "government salary exception," a public official
always has an economic interest in his or herpersonal finances, and may have a conflict
of interest in any decision resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal expenses,
income, assets or liabilities of the oflicial or his or her immdiate family. (Section
87 103 ; regulation I 8703.5.)
Step 4. Is the public official's economic interest in his personal linances directly.or
indirectly involved in the decision?
"A public official or his or her immediate family is deemed to be directly
involved in a governmental decision which has any financial effect on his or her personal
finances or those of his or her immediate family." (Section 87fi3;regulation 15704.5.)
According to this regulation, your personal finances are directly involved in the decisions
you would make as a member of the Board of Trustees if there will be any effect on yogr
salary.
Steps 5 and 6. Is there a material and foreseeable financial effect on your economic
interest in your personal finances?
Once a public official identifies his or her relevant economic interests, the official
must evaluate whether the decision will have a material financial effect on any of those
economic interests. The official must find the applicable materiality standard in
Commission regulations. (Section 87103; regulation 18700(bX5), regulation 18705, et
seq.) In addition, the official must determine if the material financial effect is
foreseeable. An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable,, if
there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 1S706(a).) Whether the
financial consequences of a goveflrmental decision are substantially likeiy at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) I Fppc ops. l9g.)


File No. I-05-056
Page No. 4
Pursuant to regulation 18705.5, "the financial effects of a decision which affects
only the salary, per diem, or reimbursernent for expenses the public official or a member
of his or her immediate farnily receives from a federal, state, or local government agency
shall not be deerned material, unless the decision is to hire, fire, promote, demote,
suspend without pay or otherwise take disciplinary action with financial sanction against
the official or a member of his or her immediate family, or to set a salary for the official
or a member of his or her immediate family which is different from salaries paid to other
employees of the government agency in the same job classification or position."
Therefore, if you were elected to the Board of Trustees, you would not have a
conflict of interest so long as your decisions do not involve hiring, firing, promoting,
demoting, suspending without pay or disciplinary action with financial sanction against
you or a member of your immediate family, or to set a salary for you or a mernber of your
immediate family which is different from salaries paid to other employees in the same job
classifi cation or position.
StepsT&8. Exceptions
Even if you determine that a decision would have areasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on one or more of your economic interests, you may still
participate in the decisions if the effect is not distinguishable from the effeci on the public
generally. However, you have not suggested to us that the public generally exception
would apply to the decisions in question here.
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, you may be called upon to take part in
a decision despite the fact that you may have a disqualifuing conflict of interest. This
"legally required participation" rule applies only in certain very specific circumstances
where the government agency would beparalyzed from acting. You have not indicated
this exception will apply.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
^5'uoLJ 6"--
By: Sukhdip Brar
Intem, Legal Division
SB:rd
